title: DNS Security
description: Threats to DNS infrastructure and defenses including DNSSEC, DoH, DoT, and monitoring.
fullExplanation: DNS is a critical but historically insecure protocol. Queries are sent in plaintext UDP — anyone on the network can see what sites you're visiting and potentially intercept or modify responses. DNS attacks range from cache poisoning (injecting fake records) to DNS tunneling (exfiltrating data through DNS queries) to DDoS amplification (using DNS servers as amplifiers). Modern defenses include DNSSEC (integrity), DoH/DoT (privacy), and DNS monitoring (threat detection).

attacks.description: Inject fake DNS records into resolver cache — redirect users to attacker's server
Send small queries with spoofed source IP — large responses flood victim (up to 100x amplification)
Encode data in DNS queries/responses to exfiltrate data or bypass firewalls
Compromise DNS server or registrar to change authoritative records
Flood resolver with queries for non-existent domains — exhaust resolver resources
Dangling CNAME points to unclaimed cloud resource — attacker claims it

defenses.description: Cryptographic signatures on DNS records — prevents cache poisoning
Encrypts DNS in HTTPS on port 443 — prevents eavesdropping and interception
Encrypts DNS with TLS on port 853 — privacy without HTTPS overhead
DNS firewall — block malicious domains at resolver level
Limit DNS responses per source IP — mitigates amplification attacks
Detect DNS tunneling, DGA domains, unusual query patterns

keyFacts: DNS queries are plaintext UDP — ISPs and attackers can see every domain you visit
DoH (port 443): encrypted DNS — looks like HTTPS traffic, hard to block
DoT (port 853): encrypted DNS — easy to block/monitor by network admins
DNS tunneling tools: iodine, dnscat2 — encode TCP/data in DNS TXT/CNAME records
DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm): malware generates random domains for C2 — hard to block
Quad9 (9.9.9.9): DNS resolver that blocks malicious domains
